steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the text "most strongly suggests" about how nonvisible light analysis can inform attribution judgments. The passage gives two examples: (1) Monet's Wisteria revealed a hidden earlier step in his process; (2) detecting titanium oxide can expose forgeries. The question links both to the attribution question.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — It can provide new information about the attributed artist's practices.: The Monet example shows a previously unknown step (painting over water lilies) — new information about his working method. The forgery example also reveals information relevant to who made the work. This matches both examples. ✓
  • Choice B — It can definitively undermine that attribution.: The forgery example can rule out a claimed attribution, but the passage says "in some cases" — not definitively or always. Too strong. ✗
  • Choice C — It can confirm that attribution with a high degree of confidence.: The passage describes detecting forgeries (undermining attribution) and revealing artistic process — not confirming who painted something. ✗
  • Choice D — It can show how the work is similar to others attributed to the same artist.: The passage never discusses comparing multiple works by the same artist. Not supported. ✗
A is correct.
